lesson.name: Pure Math Specialty — Linear Algebra, Real Analysis, Topology - Lesson 3 — A Primer of Real Analysis

meta.courseTitle: Pure Math Specialty — Linear Algebra, Real Analysis, Topology

messages.content:
This summary covers the provided excerpt regarding **Fundamentals of Real Analysis**, specifically focusing on the mathematical development of the real number system and the motivation for shifting from rational numbers to real numbers.

### Summary: Foundations of Real Analysis

The core mission of the text is to provide a rigorous, proof-based foundation for concepts typically encountered in calculus.

#### 1. Mathematical Framework
*   **Integers:** The text assumes the existence of the set of integers ($\mathbb{Z}$) and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic as the starting point [S.9].
*   **Set Theory:** A naive approach to sets is used, defining objects by property $p$ [S.9]. It introduces fundamental operations (union, intersection, difference) and the **Cartesian product** [S.9, S.10].
*   **Relations and Functions:** 
    *   **Equivalence Relations:** Defined by reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity [S.11].
    *   **Functions:** Defined formally as relations where each element in the domain maps to exactly one element in the range, including concepts of one-to-one, onto, and inverse functions [S.11, S.12].
    *   **Axiom of Choice:** Acknowledged as the foundation for the existence of "choice functions" [S.12].

#### 2. The Development of Rational Numbers ($\mathbb{Q}$)
*   **Construction:** Rational numbers are defined as equivalence classes of pairs of integers $(p, q)$ where $q \neq 0$ [S.12].
*   **Field Properties:** The text demonstrates that $\mathbb{Q}$ forms an **ordered field**, satisfying standard arithmetic properties (addition, multiplication) and exhibiting order properties (e.g., $a < b$) [S.13, S.14].
*   **Metric Properties:** By defining absolute value, the text introduces distance $d(a, b) = |a-b|$. It establishes the **Triangle Inequality** and the **Archimedean property** of rational numbers [S.15].

#### 3. Limitations of Rational Numbers
*   **Lack of Completeness:** The text highlights a critical flaw in $\mathbb{Q}$: it contains "holes." There is no rational number whose square is 2 (or 3 or 6) [S.16, S.17].
*   **Sequences:** A sequence $\{a_i\}$ is defined as a function. A sequence is **Cauchy** if the terms eventually become arbitrarily close to one another [S.18].
*   **Convergence Gap:** While all convergent sequences are Cauchy sequences, not all Cauchy sequences in $\mathbb{Q}$ converge to a rational number [S.18, S.19]. This necessitates the construction of the **Real Numbers ($\mathbb{R}$)** [S.20].

#### 4. The Transition to Real Numbers ($\mathbb{R}$)
*   $\mathbb{R}$ is defined as the set of all equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences of rational numbers. This construction fills the gaps left by the rational number system, ensuring the "completeness" required for the rigorous study of calculus [S.20].

Lesson 3 — A Primer of Real Analysis - Presentation4 slides
A Primer of Real Analysis
  • Laying the foundations for rigorous calculus.
  • Exploring sets, functions, and the real numbers.

Here is a summary of the provided text:

### 1. Fundamentals (Sets and Relations)
Mathematics begins with the set of integers $\mathbb{Z}$. Sets allow for the collection of objects via defined properties, with operations including union ($\cup$), intersection ($\cap$), and difference ($\setminus$). A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is defined as a subset of $A \times A$.
*   **Equivalence Relations:** A relation is an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. These relations partition a set into disjoint equivalence classes $[x]$.
*   **Functions:** A function $f: A \rightarrow B$ is a relation where each element in the domain $A$ maps to exactly one element in the codomain $B$. Functions can be one-to-one (injective) or onto (surjective).

### 2. Rational Numbers ($\mathbb{Q}$)
Rational numbers are defined as equivalence classes of ordered pairs $(p, q)$ of integers, representing fractions $p/q$.
*   **Field Properties:** The set $\mathbb{Q}$ satisfies the field axioms, including commutativity, associativity, distributive properties, and the existence of additive/multiplicative inverses.
*   **Order and Absolute Value:** $\mathbb{Q}$ is an ordered field. The absolute value $|a|$ defines a metric $d(a, b) = |a - b|$, allowing for the expression of the triangle inequality: $|a - b| \leq |a - c| + |c - b|$. The field is Archimedean, meaning for any positive $a, b$, there exists $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ such that $na > b$.

### 3. Bounds and Convergence
*   **Supremum and Infimum:** While some subsets of $\mathbb{Q}$ have upper bounds, they do not necessarily have a least upper bound (supremum) within $\mathbb{Q}$. For example, the set of rational numbers whose square is less than 2 does not have a supremum in $\mathbb{Q}$ because no rational number satisfies $s^2 = 2$.
*   **Sequences:** A sequence is a function from a subset of $\mathbb{Z}$ to a set. A sequence $\{a_i\}$ converges to a limit $L$ if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that $|a_i - L| < \epsilon$ for all $i > N$.
*   **Cauchy Sequences:** A sequence is Cauchy if the terms eventually become arbitrarily close to one another: $|a_i - a_k| < \epsilon$ for sufficiently large $i, k$. While all convergent sequences are Cauchy sequences, the converse is not true in the rational number system (pp. 11-19).

The provided text covers the construction of real numbers via Cauchy sequences of rationals and their properties as an ordered field. Key points include:

**Algebraic Properties of Real Numbers**
Real numbers are defined as equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences of rational numbers. Addition and multiplication are defined by operating on these sequences. Proving these operations are well-defined involves showing that if $a_i \sim c_i$ and $b_i \sim d_i$, then the results for their sums and products remain equivalent ($a_i+b_i \sim c_i+d_i$ and $a_ib_i \sim c_id_i$) (p. 21-22). The construction confirms that the set of real numbers $R$ satisfies the axioms of a field, including the existence of additive inverses and multiplicative inverses for all non-zero elements (p. 22-24).

**Order and Topology**
- The real numbers form an ordered field where for any $a, b$, one and only one of $a < b$, $a = b$, or $a > b$ is true (p. 24).
- The absolute value function $|a|$ induces a metric $d(a, b) = |a - b|$, satisfying the triangle inequality $|a + b| \leq |a| + |b|$ (p. 25).
- $R$ is an Archimedean ordered field, meaning that for any $a, b$ with $0 < a < b$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $na > b$ (p. 26).
- Between any two distinct real numbers, there exists at least one rational number (p. 26).

**Supremum and Completeness**
A crucial result is the Least Upper Bound Property: every non-empty set of real numbers that has an upper bound possesses a supremum (least upper bound) in $R$ (p. 27). This property serves as the foundation for defining limits of sequences.

**Sequences and Convergence**
- A sequence $\{a_i\}$ converges to $L$ if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that $|a_i - L| < \epsilon$ for all $i > N$ (p. 29).
- A monotonic, bounded sequence of real numbers always converges. Specifically, a nondecreasing, bounded sequence converges to its supremum (p. 29).
- Extended real numbers include $+\infty$ and $-\infty$. Operations are extended to these symbols (e.g., $r + \infty = \infty$ for finite $r$), though they do not form a field (p. 30-31).
- The limit superior ($\limsup$) and limit inferior ($\liminf$) are defined to characterize the behavior of sequences that may not converge to a single limit, utilizing the supremum and infimum of the tails of the sequence (p. 31).

Chapter 3, covering pages 39 to 46, focuses on the concept of cardinality and the size of sets.

Binary Representations (p. 39)
The text introduces binary representations as a foundational tool for investigating cardinality. This section sets the stage by showing how real numbers within specific intervals can be represented through sequences of 0s and 1s, which provides a bridge to understanding the size of subsets of the real numbers.

Countable and Uncountable Sets (p. 41)
This section distinguishes between different levels of infinity. It formally defines what it means for a set to be countable (having a correspondence with the set of natural numbers) versus uncountable. The text establishes that while the set of rational numbers is countable, the real numbers are inherently uncountable, demonstrating that some infinities are strictly larger than others.

Power Sets (p. 43)
The discussion of power sets explores the set of all possible subsets of a given set, denoted as $P(A)$. Cantor's theorem is emphasized here, which states that the power set of any set $A$ has a strictly greater cardinality than $A$ itself. This provides a mechanism for generating sets of increasingly large, infinite cardinalities, moving beyond the countability of the integers.

Chapter 4 explores the topology of the real line, covering the fundamental structures of sets within the real numbers. The following key points summarize the content:

Intervals (p. 47)
Intervals represent a specific subset of real numbers defined by their endpoints. They are the building blocks of the topology of the real line, including bounded intervals (open, closed, or half-open) and unbounded intervals (such as rays or the entire real line).

Open Sets (p. 48)
A set is considered open if, for every point within the set, there exists an interval centered at that point that is entirely contained within the set. Open sets serve as the foundation for defining continuity and other top-level concepts in analysis.

Closed Sets (p. 49)
A set is defined as closed if its complement—the set of all points in the real line not in the original set—is an open set. Closely related to this concept are limit points, where a set is closed if and only if it contains all of its limit points.

Compact Sets (p. 54)
Compactness is a vital property for proving the existence of extrema and uniform continuity. A set is defined as compact if it is both closed and bounded. The document establishes that compact sets in the real line possess the heuristic property that any sequence contained within the set has a subsequence converging to a point within that same set.

The section spanning pages 59 to 80 covers the foundational concepts of limits, continuity, and their properties in real analysis.

Limits

The theory of limits (p. 59) is central to the analysis of functions. This includes the behavior of polynomials and rational functions (p. 63), as well as equivalent definitions of limits (p. 64). The text further explores limits at infinity and infinite limits (p. 69).

Continuity

A function is continuous at a point if its limit as it approaches that point equals the function's value (p. 70). The discussion extends to continuity over a set (p. 73) and several major theorems:

The Intermediate Value Theorem (p. 75) states that if $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, then $f$ takes on every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$.

The Extreme Value Theorem (p. 76) establishes that a continuous function on a closed, bounded interval attains its maximum and minimum values.

Uniform continuity (p. 77) is introduced as a stronger property where the value of $\delta$ depends only on $\epsilon$ and not on the specific point in the domain.

Chapter 6 explores the theory of derivatives, focusing on linear approximations and the properties of differentiable functions.

Best Linear Approximations
The derivative represents the best linear approximation of a function near a point. A function $f$ is differentiable if there exists a linear map $L(h) = ah$ such that the difference between the function increment and the linear map vanishes faster than the increment $h$ itself.

Derivatives
The derivative $f'(a)$ is defined as the unique value $a$ satisfying the limit $\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}$. Key rules for differentiation, such as the sum, product, and chain rules, are established based on this formal definition.

The Mean Value Theorem
Central to this chapter are Rolle’s theorem and the Mean Value Theorem. Rolle’s theorem states that if a continuous function has equal values at the endpoints of an interval, its derivative must be zero at some point within that interval. The Mean Value Theorem generalizes this, implying that for any differentiable function on $[a, b]$, there exists a point $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$.

Discontinuities and Taylor's Theorem
The text notes that derivatives can be discontinuous. It concludes by introducing Taylor’s Theorem, which provides a method for approximating functions using higher-order derivatives. This includes the formulation of Taylor polynomials and the analysis of the remainder term to understand the error of these approximations.

The content spanning pages 99–122 focuses on the formal development of integration and its core theorems.

**Integrals**
Integration is developed through upper and lower integrals. For a function defined on an interval, these values are used to determine if the function is integrable. When the upper and lower integrals coincide for a given function, that common value defines the integral of the function over the specified interval.

**Integrability and Properties**
The text establishes specific conditions under which a function is considered integrable. It also outlines essential properties of integrals, including how they behave linearly and over adjacent intervals. These sections include extensions of integral definitions to cover broader scenarios.

**Fundamental Theorem of Calculus**
The relationship between differentiation and integration is formalized through the following results:
1. One version of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus relates the integral of a function to its antiderivative.
2. The alternative version of the theorem establishes that the definite integral of a function, when viewed as a function of its upper limit, is differentiable, and its derivative is the original integrand.

**Advanced Applications**
Taylor’s Theorem is revisited within the context of integral remainders. Additionally, the text addresses improper integrals, providing a framework for evaluating integrals where the interval or the function is unbounded, demonstrating the extensibility of the integration theory developed in the chapter.

Chapter 8, "More Functions" (pp. 123-141), provides a formal analysis of several transcendental and trigonometric functions, establishing their properties and calculus-related behavior.

The arctangent and tangent functions are discussed first. The arctangent function is defined, and the tangent function is introduced (pp. 123-125).

The text provides a rigorous approach to the sine and cosine functions (pp. 128-133). It outlines their essential properties and examines their derivatives and integral properties. Specifically, it confirms that the derivative of $\sin(x)$ is $\cos(x)$ and the derivative of $\cos(x)$ is $-\sin(x)$.

The chapter concludes with the logarithm and exponential functions (pp. 136-138). The logarithm function is presented, followed by the exponential function, which is defined as its inverse. These sections establish the fundamental relationship between these two functions and their behavior in the context of analysis.

The provided document, *A Primer of Real Analysis* by Dan Sloughter, contains a table of contents and an introductory chapter on the fundamentals of mathematics. Please note that the document itself concludes at approximately page 142 (the Index). As the requested area (pages 142–152) is outside the scope of the provided text, I will summarize the foundational principles established in the document’s *Fundamentals* chapter.

### Foundations of Real Analysis
The text builds the real number system starting from the integers ($\mathbb{Z}$), assuming standard algebraic properties and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

### Sets and Set Operations
The document adopts a naive view of sets, where sets are defined by properties. Key operations include:
* Union: $A \cup B = \{x : x \in A \text{ or } x \in B\}$
* Intersection: $A \cap B = \{x : x \in A \text{ and } x \in B\}$
* Difference: $A \setminus B = \{x : x \in A, x \notin B\}$
* Cartesian Product: $A \times B = \{(a, b) : a \in A, b \in B\}$

### Relations
A relation $R$ is defined as a subset of the Cartesian product $A \times B$. The notation $a \sim b$ serves as a shorthand for $(a, b) \in R$. An example provided is the divisibility relation, where an integer $m$ relates to $n$ if there exists an integer $i$ such that $n = mi$.

### Scope of the Text
The document serves as an introduction to real analysis, covering sequences, series, cardinality, topology of the real line, limits, continuity, derivatives, integrals, and specific transcendental functions. It assumes the reader has previous experience with calculus and mathematical proofs.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Regarding the relation R on the set of integers Z defined by 'm divides n' (m ~R n if n = mi for some integer i), which of the following is true?
options: The relation is reflexive and transitive, but not symmetric.
The relation is symmetric and transitive, but not reflexive.
The relation is an equivalence relation.
The relation is only defined for positive integers.
questions.explanation: As noted in Exercise 1.1.1, the relation is reflexive (m = m*1) and transitive (if m divides n and n divides k, then m divides k), but it is not symmetric because 2 divides 4, but 4 does not divide 2.

questions.question: Why is the definition of addition on the rational numbers Q (p/q + s/t = (pt+sq)/qt) considered well-defined?
options: Because it is derived from the properties of prime factorization.
Because the result is independent of the representative chosen for the equivalence classes.
Because Q is defined as a subset of the integers Z.
Because every rational number has a unique representation as p/q where q > 0 and gcd(p,q)=1.
questions.explanation: Section 1.3.1 demonstrates that if you pick different representatives for the same equivalence classes (p,q) ~ (a,b), the resulting sum is always in the same equivalence class, ensuring the operation is well-defined.

questions.question: What is the primary significance of the statement 'A subset R of A x B is a function if for every a in A there exists one and only one b in B such that (a, b) in R'?
options: It restricts functions to only involve sets of integers.
It ensures that a function can always be inverted.
It provides a rigorous definition of a function that maps each domain element to a unique range element.
It proves that all functions must be onto the set B.
questions.explanation: This definition provides the formal, set-theoretic foundation for what constitutes a function, ensuring that 'f(a)=b' is unambiguous for every element in the domain.

questions.question: In the context of the proof of Proposition 1.3.1 (|a+b| <= |a| + |b|), what role does the property -|a| <= a <= |a| play?
options: It allows for the conversion of absolute value inequalities into linear inequalities.
It proves that the rational numbers are a field.
It serves as a counterexample for negative numbers.
It is unnecessary for the proof, as the triangle inequality is an axiom.
questions.explanation: The proof uses the fact that -|a| <= a <= |a| and -|b| <= b <= |b| to show that the sums of these terms are non-negative, allowing the author to conclude the inequality by manipulating the expression |a| + |b| - |a+b|.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'naive view of sets' mentioned in the text?
options: Sets are only defined by enumerating their elements explicitly.
Any collection of objects defined by a property p constitutes a set.
Sets must be finite collections of integers.
A set is defined strictly as a subset of the real line.
questions.explanation: Section 1.1.2 explicitly states: 'We will take a naive view of sets: given any property p, we may determine a set by collecting together all objects which have property p'.

cards.front: What is the definition of a subset A ⊂ B?
cards.back: A ⊂ B if for every x, x ∈ A implies x ∈ B.

cards.front: What is the Cartesian product A × B?
cards.back: The set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B.

cards.front: What are the three properties of an equivalence relation R on a set A?
cards.back: 1. Reflexive (a ~ a for all a) 2. Symmetric (if a ~ b then b ~ a) 3. Transitive (if a ~ b and b ~ c then a ~ c).

cards.front: What is the equivalence class [x] of an element x given an equivalence relation R?
What does it mean for a function f: A → B to be one-to-one?
cards.back: For every b in the range of f, there exists a unique a ∈ A such that f(a) = b.

cards.front: What is the Axiom of Choice?
cards.back: The assumption that for any collection of nonempty sets {A_α}, there exists a choice function φ: I → ∪A_α such that φ(α) ∈ A_α.

cards.front: How is the rational number set Q defined as a field?
cards.back: Q is defined as the set of equivalence classes of pairs (p,q) where p,q ∈ Z and q ≠ 0, under the relation (p,q) ~ (s,t) if pt = qs.

cards.front: What are the conditions for a rational number a to be positive?
cards.back: a is positive (a ∈ Q+) if there exist p, q ∈ Z+ such that a = p/q.

cards.front: What is the triangle inequality for rational numbers?
cards.back: |a − b| ≤ |a − c| + |c − b| for all a, b, c ∈ Q.

cards.front: What does the function d(a, b) = |a − b| represent?
cards.back: It is a metric, representing the distance between a and b.

slides.title: A Primer of Real Analysis
bullets: Laying the foundations for rigorous calculus.
Exploring sets, functions, and the real numbers.
slides.notes: Welcome to our study of Real Analysis, where we transition from intuitive calculation to formal, logical rigor.

slides.title: Fundamentals: Sets and Relations
bullets: Integers serve as our foundational building blocks.
Sets are defined by property-based collection.
Relations define connections between set elements (p. 10).
Equivalence relations induce a partition of sets (p. 11).
slides.notes: We begin by defining sets naively and establishing equivalence relations, which are crucial for constructing the real numbers.

slides.title: Functions: Basic Definitions
bullets: A function is a relation assigning unique outputs.
Distinction between domain, range, and graph.
Onto mappings cover the entire target set (p. 12).
slides.notes: Functions are specialized relations that ensure each input has a strictly defined, singular output.

slides.title: Constructing Real Numbers
bullets: Real numbers defined via Cauchy sequences of rationals.
Addition and multiplication maintain Cauchy properties (p. 21).
Sequences bounded away from zero are invertible.
Equivalence classes group sequences converging to the same value (p. 23).
slides.notes: By using Cauchy sequences, we rigorously define real numbers as equivalence classes, completing the rational number system.
